local_names.romanized_local: Hēi Dòu (Chinese); Karuppu Pattani (Tamil); Kali Phaliyan (Hindi)

food_subgroup: Cooked legumes — black beans (Phaseolus vulgaris), boiled from dried. 1 cup = 172g. Very low GI (~30). Exceptionally high resistant starch (~7g per cup) and dietary fibre (~15g per cup).

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for cooked black beans (Phaseolus vulgaris, black variety). Closest parent is the general Phaseolus vulgaris food product node. URI left null to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00XQ used as closest parent for cooked black beans; no exact species-level code confirmed for black variety specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code confirmed for cooked black beans as a food item. Left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 cup of cooked black beans (approximately 172g), providing around 227 kcal, 15.3g protein, 40.8g carbohydrate, and 15.0g dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Beans, black, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173735)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values match USDA SR Legacy entry for black beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt. All per-100g values sourced directly from this entry.

gi_source.title: Black beans, cooked — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~30 (low) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for black beans, cooked. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (30 × 40.8) / 100 ≈ 12.2 per 172g serving. The very high resistant starch and soluble fibre content of black beans is the primary mechanism for the low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Near-zero sodium with very high potassium and magnesium — ideal DASH diet alignment
hypertension.guidance: Black beans cooked from dried contain only 1mg sodium per 100g (approximately 2mg per cup), with 355mg potassium and 70mg magnesium per 100g, making them one of the most hypertension-friendly protein-rich foods available and strongly aligned with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~30) and high resistant starch plus soluble fibre producing strong glycaemic dampening and second-meal effect
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of ~30 and a glycemic load of ~12.2 per cup despite 40.8g carbohydrate per serving, black beans are among the most diabetes-friendly legumes; their ~7g resistant starch per cup generates short-chain fatty acids (butyrate) via colonic fermentation that improve insulin sensitivity at subsequent meals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre, anthocyanin antioxidants, very low saturated fat, and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Black beans provide 8.7g dietary fibre per 100g (predominantly soluble), zero cholesterol, only 0.1g saturated fat per 100g, and anthocyanins (cyanidin-3-glucoside) from the black seed coat with anti-inflammatory and LDL-lowering properties, supporting cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anthocyanin anti-inflammatory activity and high folate supporting methotrexate-treated patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Anthocyanins from the black pigment (cyanidin-3-glucoside, ~80–100mg per cup) inhibit NF-κB inflammatory pathways, and the high folate content (149mcg per 100g; ~256mcg per cup = ~64% RDA) is beneficial for patients on methotrexate therapy for rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black beans (Phaseolus vulgaris, black variety) cooked from dried are deep purple-black, soft-textured legumes with an earthy, mildly sweet flavour. A standard serving is 1 cup cooked (172g), providing approximately 227 kcal, 15.3g protein, 40.8g carbohydrate, 15.0g dietary fibre (60% of daily RDA), and only 1–2mg sodium. The glycemic index is ~30 (low) with a glycemic load of ~12.2 per cup — remarkably low given the carbohydrate content — due to approximately 7g resistant starch and high soluble fibre that dramatically slow glucose absorption. The distinctive black seed coat is rich in anthocyanins (cyanidin-3-glucoside, ~80–100mg per cup) with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties comparable to blueberries. Black beans are an excellent source of folate (149mcg per 100g), potassium (355mg per 100g), magnesium (70mg per 100g), iron (2.1mg per 100g), and phosphorus (140mg per 100g). They are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free. Originating in Central and South America, they are widely used in Latin American cuisine (frijoles negros) and increasingly available in Singapore at Cold Storage and NTUC FairPrice in canned form, used in grain bowls, burritos, and health food café dishes. Less common in traditional Singapore cooking than chickpeas or mung beans but growing in popularity. Nutritionally superior to many legumes for T2DM management, hypertension, and cardiovascular health due to the combination of very low sodium, high potassium, high fibre, low GI, and anthocyanin content.
common_user_queries: are black beans good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (exact match: black beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt). GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the approved primary source.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (naturally zero — plant-based legume contains no dietary cholesterol). Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no B12). Vitamin C is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin A is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin D is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Water content (65.8g per 100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (0.04g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.22g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile. The food_subgroup notes ~7g resistant starch per cup — this is a well-documented property of cooked black beans supported by published literature but the exact per-100g resistant starch value is not separately listed in USDA SR Legacy; it is included in the total dietary fibre figure. GI glycemic load calculated as (30 × 40.8) / 100 = 12.24, rounded to 12.2. Folate reported as mcg DFE; USDA SR Legacy reports total folate as 149mcg per 100g for cooked black beans without salt. FoodOn URI left null — no specific URI confirmed for black bean variety; closest parent noted. SNOMED CT code left null — no confirmed food-item code for cooked black beans.
